title: One equals sign in an if

options: Prints nothing, since 80 is not equal to 75
It doesn't compile — the condition isn't a boolean
It always prints, because assignment succeeds
mcq.explanation_md: `attendance = 75` is an **assignment**, and in Java its result is the assigned value — an `int`. An `if` demands a `boolean`, so the compiler rejects this line outright. The tempting answer is "it always prints" — that's what happens in C, where any non-zero number counts as true. Java deliberately closed that trap: mixing up `=` (assign) and `==` (compare) becomes a compile error instead of a silent bug. One exception worth knowing: with a `boolean` variable, `if (flag = true)` *does* compile — and always runs.
